perturbation.original_text: Recipient affirms that it sha ll not and shall not allow others to take the Discloser’s Confidential Information outside the United States without express written authorization from the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient affirms that it shall not and shall not allow others to take the Discloser’s Confidential Information outside the United States without express written authorization from the Discloser. However, for the purposes of data backup and disaster recovery, Recipient is authorized to store encrypted copies of the Confidential Information on servers located in Canada.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction regarding the location of data. Section 7 initially restricts taking Confidential Information outside the United States without explicit authorization. The added sentence then permits storing encrypted copies in Canada for data backup and disaster recovery, contradicting the absolute restriction. This creates uncertainty about whether storing data in Canada is a violation of the agreement if the Discloser hasn't provided specific authorization.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient affirms that it sha ll not and shall not allow others to take the Discloser’s Confidential Information outside the United States without express written authorization from the Discloser. However, for the purposes of data backup and disaster recovery, Recipient is authorized to store encrypted copies of the Confidential Information on servers located in Canada.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Recipient cannot take confidential information outside of the United States without express written authorization. The changed text adds an exception, allowing the storage of encrypted copies in Canada for data backup and disaster recovery. This directly contradicts the original restriction, creating a strong contradiction. The contradicted text being the same as the changed text reinforces the in-text nature of the contradiction, as the exception now exists within the same clause that initially prohibited the action. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where different sections of a contract (in this case, different parts of the same section after modification) provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon provision to the other party one (1) day written notice of election to so terminate, subject to the provisions of section 1)d) of this Agreement, provided that such unilateral termination by Recipient is without prejudice to the State of Ohio.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon provision to the other party one (1) day written notice of election to so terminate, subject to the provisions of section 1)d) of this Agreement, provided that such unilateral termination by Recipient is without prejudice to the State of Ohio. However, the Discloser reserves the right to terminate this agreement with no prior notice if the Recipient violates any confidentiality provisions.
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence introduces a conflicting termination clause. Initially, both parties require one day's written notice for termination. The added clause allows the Discloser to terminate immediately without notice if the Recipient breaches confidentiality. This in-text contradiction creates uncertainty about the proper termination procedure, particularly for the Discloser in cases of confidentiality breaches. It contradicts the established one-day notice requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement upon provision to the other party one (1) day written notice of election to so terminate, subject to the provisions of section 1)d) of this Agreement, provided that such unilateral termination by Recipient is without prejudice to the State of Ohio. However, the Discloser reserves the right to terminate this agreement with no prior notice if the Recipient violates any confidentiality provisions.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a clause allowing the Discloser to terminate the agreement without prior notice if the Recipient violates confidentiality provisions. This directly contradicts the original clause (and the contradicted text, which is identical to the original text) that states both parties require one day's written notice for termination. This creates an in-text contradiction as it introduces conflicting termination procedures, specifically for the Discloser. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition, where different sections provide conflicting obligations, leading to uncertainty about which terms should be enforced.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall return all Confidential Information , copies, extracts, or notes derived from Confidential Information to Discloser or certify, in writing, the destruc tion thereof. The provisions of this Section 1)d) shall survive the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall return all Confidential Information , copies, extracts, or notes derived from Confidential Information to Discloser or certify, in writing, the destruc tion thereof within 30 days of the termination. The provisions of this Section 1)d) shall survive the termination of this Agreement; however, for any digital copies, the Recipient may retain these copies for a period of 90 days after termination, for archival purposes.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction related to the return of confidential information after termination. Originally, the recipient must either return or certify the destruction of all confidential information upon termination, without specifying a timeframe, but stating 1)d) survives. The modified text adds a 30-day deadline for returning or certifying destruction but then contradicts itself by permitting the Recipient to retain digital copies for 90 days after termination for archival reasons. This causes confusion on when the recipient must return the infomation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Return of Confidential Information . Upon request of Discloser or termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall return all Confidential Information , copies, extracts, or notes derived from Confidential Information to Discloser or certify, in writing, the destruc tion thereof within 30 days of the termination. The provisions of this Section 1)d) shall survive the termination of this Agreement; however, for any digital copies, the Recipient may retain these copies for a period of 90 days after termination, for archival purposes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction. The original text states that all confidential information must be returned or destroyed upon termination. The changed text adds a 30-day deadline for this but then contradicts itself by allowing the recipient to retain digital copies for 90 days after termination for archival purposes. This conflicts with the requirement in the contradicted text that mandates return or destruction 'within 30 days of termination'. This creates a direct and clear conflict regarding when the recipient needs to return the digital copies, leading to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Informati on” refers to the following items Discloser discloses to the Recipient: (1) Any information that is in written, graphic, machine readable , or other tangible form, and that Discloser designates as “ Confidential, “specifically including, but not limited to , the BWC Information Systems Documentation containing file specifications and data definitions for files and reports which will be utilized under the contract to be awarded under RFP CSP903918 ; (2) Any oral or visual information Discloser designates as “Confidential” at the time of disclosure; (3) Any source code and any names of actual or potential Discloser s, whether or not marked as confidential; and (4) Any other nonpublic, sensitive or third party information that is possession of Discloser a nd is disclosed to Recipient under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Informati on” refers to the following items Discloser discloses to the Recipient: (1) Any information that is in written, graphic, machine readable , or other tangible form, and that Discloser designates as “ Confidential, “specifically including, but not limited to , the BWC Information Systems Documentation containing file specifications and data definitions for files and reports which will be utilized under the contract to be awarded under RFP CSP903918 ; (2) Any oral or visual information Discloser designates as “Confidential” at the time of disclosure; (3) Any source code and any names of actual or potential Discloser s, whether or not marked as confidential; and (4) Any other nonpublic, sensitive or third party information that is possession of Discloser a nd is disclosed to Recipient under this Agreement. However, information that has been disclosed to the public via a FOIA request will not be considered confidential.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction within the definition of 'Confidential Information.' The original definition broadly covers various forms of information disclosed by the Discloser. The added sentence excludes information disclosed through a FOIA request, creating a contradiction. While the initial definition implies a comprehensive scope of confidentiality, the added clause introduces an exception that conflicts with the breadth of the original definition. This creates uncertainty about what truly constitutes 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Definition i) “Confidential Informati on” refers to the following items Discloser discloses to the Recipient: (1) Any information that is in written, graphic, machine readable , or other tangible form, and that Discloser designates as “ Confidential, “specifically including, but not limited to , the BWC Information Systems Documentation containing file specifications and data definitions for files and reports which will be utilized under the contract to be awarded under RFP CSP903918 ; (2) Any oral or visual information Discloser designates as “Confidential” at the time of disclosure; (3) Any source code and any names of actual or potential Discloser s, whether or not marked as confidential; and (4) Any other nonpublic, sensitive or third party information that is possession of Discloser a nd is disclosed to Recipient under this Agreement. However, information that has been disclosed to the public via a FOIA request will not be considered confidential.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds an exception to the definition of 'Confidential Information' that did not exist in the original text. Specifically, it states that information disclosed through a FOIA request will not be considered confidential. This directly contradicts the broader definition of 'Confidential Information' in the contradicted text, which includes various forms of information disclosed by the Discloser, regardless of whether it was later disclosed through a FOIA request. The original text makes no exceptions based on FOIA disclosures, creating a direct conflict in what constitutes 'Confidential Information' within the document. The contradicted text is the same as the Changed text, which makes it an in-text contradiction, following the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate and prepare a response to RFP CSP903918 for a Pharmacy Benefits Manager (PBM) for the Bureau of Workers’ Compensation Pharmacy Program , issued by DAS on behalf of Discloser, as contemplated by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate and prepare a response to RFP CSP903918 for a Pharmacy Benefits Manager (PBM) for the Bureau of Workers’ Compensation Pharmacy Program , issued by DAS on behalf of Discloser, as contemplated by this Agreement. The recipient may use anonymized data extracted from confidential information for internal research purposes.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a conflict regarding the use of Confidential Information. The initial clause strictly limits the use of Confidential Information to preparing a response to the RFP. The added sentence then permits using anonymized data extracted from confidential information for internal research purposes. This directly contradicts the earlier restriction, creating uncertainty about the permissible uses of information derived from the confidential data. Even if anonymized, the allowance for internal research creates ambiguity regarding the scope of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Restrictions on Use . i) Recipient shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate and prepare a response to RFP CSP903918 for a Pharmacy Benefits Manager (PBM) for the Bureau of Workers’ Compensation Pharmacy Program , issued by DAS on behalf of Discloser, as contemplated by this Agreement. The recipient may use anonymized data extracted from confidential information for internal research purposes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a sentence allowing the use of anonymized data for internal research, which directly contradicts the original clause (and the contradicted text, which is the same as the changed text due to its placement there) that strictly limits the use of Confidential Information to preparing a response to the RFP. This creates a clear conflict and uncertainty about the permissible uses of confidential data, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree tha t the disclosure of Confidential Information of the Discloser may cause the Discloser irreparable damage for which remedies other than injunctive relief may be inadequate, and the Recipient agrees that in the event of a breach of the obligations hereunder, t he Discloser shall be entitled to temporary and permanent injunctive relief to enforce this provision without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree tha t the disclosure of Confidential Information of the Discloser may cause the Discloser irreparable damage for which remedies other than injunctive relief may be inadequate, and the Recipient agrees that in the event of a breach of the obligations hereunder, t he Discloser shall be entitled to temporary and permanent injunctive relief to enforce this provision without the necessity of proving actual damages. However, for any information already publicly available before the breach, the Discloser will not be entitled to injunctive relief.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an exception to the Discloser's right to injunctive relief. The original clause states that the Discloser is entitled to injunctive relief without proving actual damages in the event of a breach. The added sentence then states that for any information already publicly available *before* the breach, the Discloser will not be entitled to injunctive relief. It causes confusion about the circumstances where injunctive relief is available, creating uncertainty about the Discloser's remedies, and if it was public before. It is an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: Injunction . The Parties agree tha t the disclosure of Confidential Information of the Discloser may cause the Discloser irreparable damage for which remedies other than injunctive relief may be inadequate, and the Recipient agrees that in the event of a breach of the obligations hereunder, t he Discloser shall be entitled to temporary and permanent injunctive relief to enforce this provision without the necessity of proving actual damages. However, for any information already publicly available before the breach, the Discloser will not be entitled to injunctive relief.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Discloser is entitled to injunctive relief without proving actual damages in the event of a breach. The changed text introduces an exception by saying that for any information already publicly available before the breach, the Discloser will not be entitled to injunctive relief. This directly contradicts the original entitlement to injunctive relief regardless of actual damages, creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' because it provides a conflicting obligation.
